metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated a strategic business problem but never quantified financial or operational impact. On the 04/02/2025 intro call, Cory Griffiths (Dir-Procurement) stated LCS needs 'a full-suite S2P platform, preferring one provider over multiple point solutions' covering Procurement, Sourcing, Spend Analytics, Contracts, Supplier Management, Project & Savings Management (source: Next Step summary, Christian initial meeting 04/02/2025). On 06/09/2025, Cory confirmed they are 'Looking at P2P, with S2P on wishlist. Currently using DDSI but exploring other options' (source: Next Step 06/09/2025). The business driver is consolidation from fragmented point solutions to a unified platform, but no metrics were shared: no baseline cost, no cycle-time pain, no error rate, no targeted savings, no ROI case. Budget was verbally confirmed at '~$150K+ SaaS, equal or more for Services' (source: Next Step Q1-Q2 2025 summary, April 2025) but was never tied to a quantified value case or return. Without quantified impact, the CFO/Economic Buyer has no financial justification to approve $195K. The quantification gap is a blocker to executive sign-off and must be surfaced before commercial negotiation.

champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. A champion actively advocates for Zycus, provides internal intelligence, introduces Zycus to decision-makers, and navigates the buying process on Zycus's behalf. Cory Griffiths (Dir-Procurement) and Elissa Rogers (Procurement Analyst) are engaged day-to-day contacts but have demonstrated no champion behavior: they have not introduced Zycus to Rod Copple (Decision Maker, CFO) or Elisa Baptiste (SVP COO), have not shared internal vendor evaluations or competitive intelligence, have not driven urgency or escalated the deal internally, and have not advocated for Zycus over the five active competitors. The Next Step on 06/30/2026 states 'Cory was receptive on the intro call but has been quiet again. We need to decide if this is worth keeping in pipeline' (source: Next Step 06/30/2026), indicating Cory is passive, not an advocate. Cory promised RFP delivery three times (Oct 2025, Dec 2025, March 2026) and never delivered, suggesting he lacks influence or urgency. Elissa Rogers has been a meeting attendee but no tasks, emails, or Next Step entries document her advocacy. Rod Copple (Decision Maker) handed off ownership and disengaged, the opposite of championship. No other stakeholder (David Bennett, Brian Lambert, Robbie Rushing, Elisa Baptiste) has shown advocacy. Without a champion, Zycus has no internal navigation, no competitive intelligence, no access to the Economic Buyer, and no differentiation in a five-vendor competitive set. The deal is at high risk of silent loss to a competitor with better internal alignment. The next move is to explicitly recruit Cory or Elissa as a champion by offering exclusive value (early access to Merlin AI capabilities, a peer reference call with Brookdale/Benchmark, a seat at Procurement Pinnacle event, a tailored ROI model) and asking them to facilitate an executive sponsor meeting with Rod Copple or Elisa Baptiste.

competition.narrative: Five active competitors are confirmed (Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba, iCertis) plus the incumbent DDSI, creating a six-way competitive evaluation with no clear Zycus wedge or positioning strategy. The Salesforce Competitors field lists 'Coupa;iValua;JAGGAER;SAP Ariba;iCertis' (source: SF Competitor field). The Next Step on 06/09/2025 states 'Currently using DDSI but exploring other options' (source: Next Step 06/09/2025), confirming DDSI as the incumbent P2P provider and the do-nothing / status-quo competitor. The Next Step on 10/24/2025 states 'They are still evaluating other vendors with high-level overview demos' (source: Next Step 10/24/2025), confirming the buyer is running a broad multi-vendor evaluation with high-level demos (likely all five named competitors plus Zycus). No competitive intelligence has been gathered since the September 2025 demo: no insight into which vendors the buyer favors, no discovery of competitor pricing or proposal status, no understanding of whether the RFP (promised but never delivered) was sent to all six vendors or abandoned. The demo on 09/12/2025 was 'received well' (source: Next Step 09/12/2025) but no shortlist confirmation followed, suggesting either (a) all vendors are still in play, or (b) the buyer has informally shortlisted and Zycus is unaware of its status. Zycus's differentiation wedge was articulated in April 2025: senior living references (Brookdale, Benchmark) and a full-suite S2P platform versus point solutions (source: Next Step Q1-Q2 2025 summary). However, this wedge has not been reinforced since, and the Merlin AI / Agentic AI capabilities (listed in Products) have not been positioned as a differentiator in any documented buyer conversation. With five enterprise-grade competitors (Coupa, SAP Ariba, JAGGAER are Tier 1 S2P vendors; iValua and iCertis are strong in sourcing and contracts), Zycus is at risk of being outpositioned on brand, incumbency (if any competitor has another module at LCS), or AI narrative. The buyer's stated preference for 'one provider over multiple point solutions' (source: Next Step 04/02/2025) favors full-suite vendors (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Zycus), but without recent competitive discovery Zycus cannot confirm this advantage. The move is to run a competitive discovery call with Cory or Elissa, explicitly asking which vendors are still in consideration, what the shortlist timing is, and where Zycus stands, then reposition the Merlin AI narrative and senior living domain expertise as the wedge.

identify_pain.narrative: The buyer has articulated operational pain (fragmented point solutions, lack of integrated S2P visibility) but has not quantified financial or cycle-time impact or tied pain to a specific operational owner beyond procurement. On 04/02/2025, Cory Griffiths (Dir-Procurement) stated LCS needs 'a full-suite S2P platform, preferring one provider over multiple point solutions' (source: Next Step, Christian initial meeting 04/02/2025), confirming the pain is tool sprawl and lack of integration across Procurement, Sourcing, Spend Analytics, Contracts, Supplier Management, Project & Savings Management. On 06/09/2025, Cory said 'Currently using DDSI but exploring other options' (source: Next Step 06/09/2025), implying dissatisfaction with the incumbent DDSI P2P solution, but the specific gaps in DDSI (functionality, usability, reporting, vendor support) were not documented. The pain owner is Cory Griffiths (procurement) and Elissa Rogers (Procurement Analyst), but no finance or operational executive (CFO, COO, CPO) has articulated pain or urgency — Rod Copple (CFO) disengaged without stating a compelling event, and Elisa Baptiste (SVP COO) has never been engaged. The buyer stated the timeline is '2-3 years out for P2P replacement' on 06/09/2025 (source: Next Step 06/09/2025), which signals low urgency and tolerance for the current state, contradicting any acute pain story. No compelling event (contract expiration, M&A, regulatory deadline, audit finding, cost crisis) has been documented. The pain is confirmed at the procurement operational level but is not executive-sponsored or urgent, which explains the 23-month stall in Formal Evaluation and the unfulfilled RFP promises. The move is to escalate pain discovery to the CFO or COO level and surface a compelling event (e.g., DDSI contract renewal date, cost reduction mandate, operational inefficiency metrics) to create urgency.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec requirements, and SI/partner involvement are entirely unknown — no discussion has reached this stage. The deal is in Formal Evaluation (stage gate: understanding requirements and demo), not Vendor Selected or Negotiation, so no MSA/SOW conversation has begun. The Next Step summary from Q1-Q2 2025 states 'no RFP expected' (source: Next Step Q1-Q2 2025 summary, April 2025), implying an informal evaluation and potentially a simpler paper path, but that was contradicted by the October 2025 Next Step stating 'Making a list of requirements and a RFP. Should hear back end of October for RFP' (source: Next Step 10/24/2025) — so the buyer's process intent shifted, then the RFP never materialized. No mention of legal review, redlining, InfoSec questionnaire, ERP/systems integration requirements, or procurement approval tiers exists in any Next Step, task, or contact interaction. LCS is a senior living organization (peers: Brookdale, Benchmark per Next Step), so InfoSec and data privacy (HIPAA-adjacent, resident data) and operational continuity during implementation are likely critical gates, but none have been surfaced. No SI or implementation partner has been named. The current $195K SaaS deal likely requires a separate SOW for services ('equal or more for Services' per April 2025 budget discussion, source: Next Step Q1-Q2 2025), but services scope, statement of work, and implementation timeline have not been discussed. The paper process is a complete unknown and will be a multi-month gate once the vendor is selected, which has not yet occurred. The move is to surface the contracting and InfoSec timeline early in the next buyer conversation to pressure-test the 08/27/2026 close date realism.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process has been opaque and fragmented across multiple handoffs, with no clear buying committee or timeline validation. The Next Step history reveals a shifting ownership chain: Rod Copple (CFO, Decision Maker) was the original executive sponsor in late 2024, then handed procurement ownership to Cory Griffiths (Dir-Procurement) in Q1 2025 after Cory's onboarding (source: Next Step 'Rod Copple (CFO) handed procurement ownership to the relevant stakeholder began in February after his onboarding'). The Next Step from Q1-Q2 2025 states 'Working with Cory Griffiths (Director of Procurement) and Elissa Rogers to schedule a high-level overview for executive leadership. Invite sent for 6/4 meeting. Cory confirmed ongoing internal discussions; next steps include executive alignment, high-level overview, and subsequent demo' (source: Next Step Q1-Q2 2025 summary). This implies a multi-step process (executive alignment → overview → demo → shortlist → RFP), but the actual sequence stalled after the demo on 09/12/2025. The buyer promised to 'shortlist' after the demo (source: Next Step 09/12/2025) and deliver an RFP in October 2025, then December 2025, then March 2026 — none occurred. The Next Step on 10/24/2025 states 'They are still evaluating other vendors with high-level overview demos' (source: Next Step 10/24/2025), confirming five competitors are active but no formal vendor selection gate has been reached. No buying committee members other than Cory, Elissa, and Brian have been documented in meetings or tasks. Elisa Baptiste (SVP COO) is a contact role but has never appeared in the process. The approval chain is unknown: no confirmation of who beyond Rod Copple must sign off (CFO alone, or CFO + COO, or board?). The buyer stated the timeline is '2-3 years out for P2P replacement' on 06/09/2025 (source: Next Step 06/09/2025), but the close date is 08/27/2026 (51 days away), a fundamental mismatch never reconciled. The process appears to have informalized or stalled entirely; Zycus has no visibility into current vendor status, next gate, or decision authority.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer has articulated high-level functional scope but has not formalized decision criteria or scoring rubric. On 04/02/2025, Cory Griffiths stated LCS wants 'a full-suite S2P platform, preferring one provider over multiple point solutions' and named six areas of interest: Procurement, Sourcing, Spend Analytics, Contracts, Supplier Management, Project & Savings Management (source: Next Step, Christian initial meeting 04/02/2025). On 06/09/2025, Cory confirmed 'Looking at P2P, with S2P on wishlist' (source: Next Step 06/09/2025), indicating P2P (procure-to-pay) is the core requirement and S2P (source-to-pay) is aspirational. The Next Step on 10/24/2025 states 'They are still evaluating other vendors with high-level overview demos. Making a list of requirements and a RFP' (source: Next Step 10/24/2025), implying criteria were under development as of October 2025. However, the RFP — which would formalize those criteria — was promised in October 2025, December 2025, and March 2026 and never delivered, so the criteria remain informal and buyer-internal. The demo on 09/12/2025 was 'received well' (source: Next Step 09/12/2025) but no test cases, use-case validation, or structured evaluation has been documented. Zycus has not seen the buyer's requirements list or scoring model. Without formalized criteria, Zycus cannot position differentiation, and the evaluation may be drifting toward price or incumbent comfort (DDSI). The move is to request the internal requirements list and offer an RFP template to structure the evaluation and surface Zycus strengths (Merlin AI, senior living references).

The Avoma notes for this call are still pending generation, so direct transcript verbatim is not yet available, but the rep-captured agentic-platform notes and Gen AI commentary independently anchor the AI-led posture.

Late 2024
